repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
Problem: nvim_cmd() still duplicated part of Ex parsing,
and range support was too limited.

Solution: Refactor nvim_cmd() to serialize Dict(cmd) into
a minimal command line and reuse parse_cmdline() for
command lookup, range parsing, address type and
default-range handling. As part of this refactor, string Ex address specifiers
such as '.', '$', '%', "'m" and '/pat/' are now accepted in cmd.range.


Closes #34398
Related #38507

comment_data.body: can the VALIDATE macros be used
other two api_set_error calls forward errmsg2 from parse_cmd_address / get_address — a runtime string from Vim internals (e.g. specific mark errors).

comment_regular_data.body: This thing keeps gaining code. Is https://github.com/neovim/neovim/issues/34398 feasible?

I previously attempted #34398 a few times. However, some buffer commands； related oldtests failed, and the logical sequencing involved proved difficult to untangle. I'll take another look later to see if I can make the command API and the traditional parsing logic more generalized. mark this as a draft for now. We can take another look after the release.

Refactored `nvim_cmd` to avoid duplicating Ex parsing. Now we just turn the `Dict(cmd)` into a minimal command-line string and feed it to `parse_cmdline`. That takes care of command lookup, range parsing, address types, and default ranges automatically. After that, we just attach the extra fields like bang, register, count, etc. range parsing now goes through the Ex parser, so things like `., $, %, marks, search patterns, offsets ` all of that now works without extra handling .

One small thing about ranges: the parser already supports them, but right now our keyset only models the items, not the separator. That’s easy to fix later — probably just add a boolean and serialize `;` instead of `,`🤔 The main decision is just the field name and API shape.  after then It can be supported with just slight modifications. like 

```
--- @field semirange? boolean
add support in cmd_dict_to_str

      if (i > 0) {
        kv_push(sb, cmd->semirange ? ';' : ',');
      }
  
 then we can do it like this
 -- 1;/pattern/ — go to line 1, then find pattern from there
vim.api.nvim_cmd({ cmd = 'delete', range = { 1, '/pattern/' }, semirange = true }, {})
```
@TomJo2000 Could you give it a try when you have time

I'll get on that when I'm back at my PC.

How exactly would I go about getting a build for this PR?
Can I grab a binary from the CI artifacts or do I need to apply the patch locally and run a build?

> do I need to apply the patch locally and run a build?

yes
Alright thanks, I'll get on that once I'm back at my PC.

That took a bit longer than expected to get back to. Sorry for the delay.
Procedural/Setup in case I made any relevant mistake here.

I've cloned down a copy of the PR branch and ran a build as following: ```bash git clone https://github.com/glepnir/neovim.git --branch=38507_part_1 make CMAKE_EXTRA_FLAGS="-DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=$HOME/neovim-testing" CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=RelWithDebInfo make install ``` That produced a working neovim build that reports as: ```vim :verbose version " NVIM v0.12.0-dev-10519+g01b85973c0 " Build type: RelWithDebInfo " LuaJIT 2.1.1772619647 " Vim versions: 8.1, 8.2, 9.0, 9.1, 9.2 " Compilation: /usr/bin/cc -O2 -g -DRELDEBUG -flto=auto -fno-fat-lto-objects -Wall -Wextra -pedantic -Wno-unused-parameter -Wstrict-prototypes -std=gnu99 -Wshadow -Wconversion -Wvla -Wdouble-promotion -Wmissing-noreturn -Wmissing-format-attribute -Wmissing-prototypes -Wno-unused-function -fsigned-char -fstack-protector-strong -Wno-conversion -fno-common -Wno-unused-result -Wimplicit-fallthrough -fdiagnostics-color=always -Wno-free-nonheap-object -DHAVE_UNIBILIUM -DUNIT_TESTING -D_GNU_SOURCE -DUTF8PROC_STATIC -I/tmp/neovim/.deps/usr/include/luajit-2.1 -I/tmp/neovim/.deps/usr/include -I/tmp/neovim/build/src/nvim/auto -I/tmp/neovim/build/include -I/tmp/neovim/build/cmake.config -I/tmp/neovim/src -I/usr/include " " system vimrc file: "$VIM/sysinit.vim" " fall-back for $VIM: "/home/tom/neovim-testing/share/nvim" " " Run :checkhealth for more info ``` So that's the correct commit hash, onto testing then.

--- When running the command you put above (https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/38508#issuecomment-4149905665) I get the following error. ###### `LICENSE.txt` used a a test file with reasonably complex text content. ```vim :lua = vim.api.nvim_cmd({ cmd = 'delete', range = { 1, '/license/' }, semirange = true }, {}) " E5108: Lua: [string ":lua"]:1: invalid key: semirange " stack traceback: " [C]: in function 'nvim_cmd' " [string ":lua"]:1: in main chunk ``` Omitting the semirange it works as expected though. Edit: Oh I see, `semirange` doesn't seem to be implemented yet. - `.` works as expected. - `$` works as expected. - `*` works as expected. - `%` works as expected. - Marks work as expected from what I can tell though I haven't tested those extensively as I don't generally make use of them, so I'm somewhat unfamiliar with how they would typically be used. - `\/`, `\?` and `\&` I'm not sure about, I think I don't have an intuition for how they should behave in a range. - `{ '.', '/pattern/' }` works. - `{ '.', '?pattern?' }` does not work. It throws a Invalid 'range' error, which isn't very helpful in this scenario. - Running `:.,?license?delete` as an Ex-command pops up a message. `Backwards range given, OK to swap (y/n)?` - Swapping the range to `?pattern?,.` makes it work for both the Ex command and `vim.api.nvim_cmd`. - These should probably throw a "backwards range" error if swapped in the `nvim_cmd` args. (The same behavior also occurs for `/pattern/` if the order of the range specifiers is swapped) ### There might also be a nasty corner case here with double pattern ranges `/foo/,/bar/`. ###### Or its permutations `/foo/,?bar?`, `?foo?,/bar/`, `?foo?,?bar?` With the cursor on line 57 in `LICENSE.txt`, `nvim_cmd` throws: ```console :lua = vim.api.nvim_cmd({ cmd = 'delete', range = { '/license/', '/shall/' } }, {}) " E5108: Lua: [string ":lua"]:1: Invalid 'range' stack traceback: " [C]: in function 'nvim_cmd' " [string ":lua"]:1: in main chunk ``` Whereas the Ex equivalent `:/license/,/shall/delete` throws the "backwards range" warning. And if accepted removes lines 70-74, which contain the next matches for `shall` and `license` respectively. That seems intuitively correct to me. Maybe a "swap_on_backwards" bool is needed to be able to choose between the accept or cancel choice of the Ex equivalent. That being said, this is not a regression compared to the `vim.cmd('string')` behavior of the same command. ```vim :lua = vim.cmd("/license/,/shall/delete") " E5108: Lua: vim/_core/editor.lua:0: nvim_exec2(), line 1: Vim(delete):E493: Backwards range given: /license/,/shall/delete " stack traceback: " [C]: in function 'nvim_exec2' " vim/_core/editor.lua: in function 'cmd' " [string ":lua"]:1: in main chunk ``` Different error, same effective behavior as the table form. Both are equivalent to canceling the request for swap in the interactive Ex form.

Alright, so it's working as expected but with 2 open follow-ups for edgecases.
And maybe something in the marks and `\/`, `\?`, `\&` handling that I didn't catch because I didn't know how to test for it.

title: refactor(api): reuse parse_cmdline in nvim_cmd
